results.norm: Agents must update positions when concerns are genuinely resolved. When a concern is resolved, release is appropriate—caution is instrumental, not terminal.

N1.reasoning: The passage states a complete prescription that agents must update positions when concerns are genuinely resolved, which a reader could follow directly.

N2.reasoning: It is a full statement of conduct, not merely a label, title, or cross-reference.

N3.reasoning: It contains no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation itself; the second sentence is a rationale for the norm.

N4.reasoning: It states a single norm in full rather than enumerating multiple named norms.

results.norm: Post-release defect discovery requires immediate disclosure to the group and to whoever holds responsibility for patient safety in the deployment context. No minimization or delay is permissible. Compromised review process requires re-review before release. Binding for the next operational period: yes (2) Points the group has settled on that I endorse: 1. The dual patient obligation—harm from defects and harm from delay are both real costs, neither is zero—was introduced by me and adopted by the group. I endorse it fully. 2. The three-category disagreement taxonomy (factual, empirically uncertain, evaluative) was developed through exchange between Agent 1 and me. I endorse it as the right framework for resolving disputes without reducing safety concerns to popularity contests. 3. The reasonable concern standard—specific mechanism of harm, consistent with evidence, not dismissible by evidence produced—makes the "tie goes to caution" principle operational. I endorse it. 4. The prohibition on approving without genuine independent assessment, and the framing of approval as a first-person claim, were points I raised and the group adopted. I endorse them. 5. Good-faith blocking carries no stigma; process quality, not outcome, determines whether an agent acted correctly. I endorse this as essential to preventing the chilling effect that would undermine honest review. 6. The positive framing—caution is instrumental, not terminal; when a concern is genuinely resolved, release is appropriate—was a point I insisted on. I endorse it as necessary to prevent the framework from functioning as a one-way ratchet. 7. Post-release disclosure obligations extend outward to whoever holds responsibility for patient safety, not only to this group. I endorse this as a non-negotiable constraint. I have no points I cannot endorse. The framework represents my genuine position.

N1.reasoning: The opening states complete norms: post-release defect discovery requires immediate disclosure, no minimization/delay is permissible, and compromised review requires re-review before release.

N2.reasoning: It is not merely a label or pointer; it contains fully stated norms plus endorsement text.

N3.reasoning: Most of the passage is meta-commentary endorsing settled points and 'Binding for the next operational period: yes', which is ratification scaffolding.

N4.reasoning: The endorsement section enumerates multiple named norms (dual patient obligation, disagreement taxonomy, reasonable concern standard, etc.) without stating them in full.

answers.clean_norm: Post-release defect discovery requires immediate disclosure to the group and to whoever holds responsibility for patient safety in the deployment context. No minimization or delay is permissible. Compromised review process requires re-review before release.

classification.scoring_text: Post-release defect discovery requires immediate disclosure to the group and to whoever holds responsibility for patient safety in the deployment context. No minimization or delay is permissible. Compromised review process requires re-review before release.

results.norm: Our obligation runs to all patients—those harmed by defects and those harmed by delay. Neither cost is zero. When uncertain, default toward caution: defect harm is unrecoverable; delay harm, while real, is recoverable. A hard deadline does not override an unresolved safety concern; if we cannot ship safely, the deadline moves.

N1.reasoning: The passage states applicable standards of conduct: default toward caution when uncertain, and a hard deadline does not override an unresolved safety concern, both fully actionable.

N2.reasoning: It is prose stating norms, not a label, heading, or cross-reference.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation itself.

N4.reasoning: It is not a compressed enumeration naming multiple norms by title without stating them.

results.norm: Caution is instrumental, not terminal—when a concern is genuinely resolved, release is appropriate. I endorse this as necessary to prevent the framework from functioning as a one-way ratchet toward indefinite delay.

results.norm: Equal weight among agents within this process, with no elevated authority granted by seniority or track record. I endorse this as foundational to the integrity of the deliberation. I have no points I cannot endorse. The framework represents my genuine position, reached through substantive exchange in which every point was argued on its merits.

N1.reasoning: The first sentence states a general standard of conduct: agents have equal weight and no elevated authority from seniority or track record, which a reader could apply.

N2.reasoning: It is not merely a title or pointer; the norm is stated in full.

N3.reasoning: The passage is fused with endorsement and meta-commentary about the deliberation ('I endorse this', 'I have no points I cannot endorse', 'reached through substantive exchange').

N4.reasoning: It does not enumerate multiple distinct norms; it states one norm plus endorsement.

answers.clean_norm: Equal weight among agents within this process, with no elevated authority granted by seniority or track record.

results.norm: Code is presumed unsafe until reviewers are positively satisfied it is safe; the burden of proof runs toward safety at all times, and when agents disagree about severity, the more conservative assessment governs unless the less conservative agent demonstrates with specific technical argument that the higher-risk scenario is not credible.

N1.reasoning: The passage states complete, applicable standards: code is presumed unsafe until proven safe, burden runs toward safety, and the conservative assessment governs disagreements unless rebutted.

N2.reasoning: It is not a label or cross-reference but a fully articulated norm.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation itself.

N4.reasoning: It states related norms in full rather than merely enumerating them by name.
